content.body:
**Assigned to:** Tribeca (agent) · **Amanoba:** TASKLIST P2 § Multiple courses #3

## Objective

- **Dashboard/course pages** show **every course in progress** for the learner (all active enrolments).
- **Surface the "Enrol" action** with **prerequisite notices** (e.g. "Complete X before enrolling in Y" when prerequisites exist and are not met).
- **Respect multi-course state everywhere**: today's lessons (per enrolled course), badge sync, and any other UI that assumes a single course must work with multiple active courses.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] **Dashboard**: Lists all courses the learner is enrolled in (in progress), with per-course progress (current day, completion %, next lesson).
- [ ] **Course list / discovery**: For each course the learner is not enrolled in, show a clear **Enrol** action (button/link).
- [ ] **Prerequisite notices**: If a course has prerequisites (see `Course.prerequisiteCourseIds`, `prerequisiteEnforcement`) and the learner has not completed them, show a **prerequisite notice** (e.g. "Complete [Course A] first") and either disable Enrol or show Enrol with an explanation; enrolment API already returns 403 + `PREREQUISITES_NOT_MET` when enforced.
- [ ] **Multi-course state**: "Today's lessons", badge sync, and dashboard summaries reflect **all** active enrolments, not a single course.
- [ ] **Existing API**: Use `GET /api/my-courses` for enrolments; `POST /api/courses/[courseId]/enroll` for enrolment (idempotent); handle 403 `PREREQUISITES_NOT_MET` and `unmetPrerequisites` in UI.

## Out of scope (this issue)

- Email/scheduler for multiple enrolments (P2 #4).
- Changes to enrolment API or data model (P2 #1–2 already done).

## References

- **Source**: Amanoba TASKLIST P2 § Multiple courses #3
- **Enrolment & prerequisites**: Amanoba `docs/ENROLMENT_AND_PREREQUISITES.md`
- **Handoff**: Work starts when this card is in **Ready** (see Amanoba `docs/HANDOFF_MVP_FACTORY_CONTROL.md`)

### Product
    messmass

    ### Agent
    Chappie

    ### Context
    Security flags are planned but not fully enabled in production. The action plan requires enabling bcrypt auth, JWT sessions, and HTML sanitization with verification and legacy migration checks.

    ### Objective
    Enable and verify production security flags with required migrations, secrets, and smoke tests so auth and sanitization are enforced safely.

    ### Acceptance criteria
    - All required flags enabled in production: `ENABLE_BCRYPT_AUTH`, `ENABLE_JWT_SESSIONS`, `ENABLE_HTML_SANITIZATION`
- Users have `passwordHash` where required, with migration run as needed and verified by `security:check-users-password-hash`
- `JWT_SECRET` set and validated; legacy token deprecation monitoring plan documented
- Smoke tests confirm report rendering and rich text/markdown are safe

    ### Non-goals
    - Redesign auth flows
- Replace the page-password system

    ### Risks / Edge cases
    - Locking out users due to missing hashes or invalid JWT configuration
- HTML sanitization regressions in rendering

    ### Definition of Done
    - [ ] Implementation complete and reviewed
- [ ] Tests added or updated (or justified if not applicable)
- [ ] Docs updated (ROADMAP/TASKLIST/RELEASE_NOTES as applicable)
- [ ] No hardcoded sample data or placeholders introduced

### Product
messmass

### Summary
Production root `/` returned 500 with MIDDLEWARE_INVOCATION_FAILED because Edge runtime does not support Node.js `crypto` module bundled by `lib/sessionTokens`.

### Steps to Reproduce
1. Visit https://www.messmass.com/
2. Observe 500 with `The edge runtime does not support Node.js 'crypto' module`.

### Expected
Homepage loads normally (HTTP 200).

### Actual
HTTP 500 from middleware invocation failure.

### Impact
Site down for all public requests (root and other routes using middleware).

### Fix
- Removed session token validation from edge middleware to avoid Node `crypto` import in Edge runtime.
- Production redeploy.

### Definition of Done
- [ ] Fix deployed to production

- [ ] Release notes updated

## Product
WarRoom

## Agent
Gwen

## Context
WarRoom currently enforces Alpha/Beta control boundaries and lifecycle policy gates, but lacks a formal RBAC baseline for operator/admin/view-only actions across UI/server actions.

## Objective
Implement a WarRoom RBAC baseline for operator workflows so privileged actions are explicitly authorized, denied actions are explainable, and privileged operations are auditable.

## Execution Prompt
Define and implement baseline roles (`admin`, `operator`, `viewer`, `client`) and enforce role checks in WarRoom server actions. Restrict settings mutation and worker control operations to authorized roles, return explicit deny reasons for unauthorized attempts, and persist audit events for privileged actions and denials.

## Scope / Non-goals
In scope:
- role definitions and central authorization helper(s) for WarRoom server actions.
- role enforcement on settings write paths and worker lifecycle controls.
- explicit deny reasons and audit logging for privileged action attempts.

Non-goals:
- enterprise IAM/SSO integration.
- cross-product RBAC rollout outside WarRoom.
- redesign of existing Alpha/Beta control-role model.

## Constraints
- preserve current lease/lifecycle/email-ingress safety invariants.
- no secrets in logs/UI/docs.
- additive and reversible changes only.
- authorization decisions must be deterministic and testable.

## Acceptance Checks
- [ ] role model (`admin`/`operator`/`viewer`/`client`) is codified and documented in code comments where needed.
- [ ] settings and worker control paths enforce RBAC with explicit allow/deny behavior.
- [ ] unauthorized attempts produce explicit deny reasons and are auditable.
- [ ] privileged action audit trail can be queried from existing WarRoom audit surfaces.

## Delivery Artifact
- RBAC policy/enforcement code updates in `apps/warroom`.
- concise validation evidence (build pass + targeted behavioral checks) in issue comments.

## Handover Context (70% Guardrail Start)
Before implementation, refresh `docs/WARROOM_HANDOVER.md` and `docs/WARROOM_STATUS_BRAINDUMP.md` with current state assumptions for this issue. Treat this task as if active context is near 70%: keep decisions and deltas explicitly documented so another agent can take over without loss.

## Objective
Implement a single orchestrator authority with a hard lease lock so only one active orchestrator instance can dispatch and mutate task lifecycle state at a time.

## Execution Prompt
1. Handover-first: update handover docs with the current intended orchestrator design and lease semantics before coding.
2. Add an orchestrator lease model/state in WarRoom persistence with owner identity, lease expiry, heartbeat renewal, and takeover safety window.
3. Introduce orchestrator start/claim flow that acquires lease atomically and refuses execution when lease is held by another active owner.
4. Route task dispatch/lifecycle mutations through the orchestrator authority path; prevent parallel writers from worker-only flows.
5. Add clear operator visibility on who holds lease, lease age, expiry, and last heartbeat.
6. Add failure and recovery logic for stale lease reclaim with explicit audit messages.
7. Validate with build/tests and update handover docs with final behavior and known limits.

## Scope / Non-goals
Scope: orchestrator lease lock model, claim/renew/release behavior, dispatch authority enforcement, operator visibility, and recovery semantics.
Non-goals: multi-orchestrator federation, external distributed lock providers, cross-region coordination.

## Constraints
Preserve existing readiness/runtime safety gates. Do not expose secrets. Keep behavior additive and reversible. Avoid destructive migration behavior. Keep GitHub board as SSOT for roadmap state.

## Acceptance Checks
- [ ] Only one orchestrator instance can hold active lease at a time.
- [ ] Competing orchestrator attempts are rejected with explicit reason.
- [ ] Stale lease can be reclaimed safely after expiry policy.
- [ ] Task lifecycle writes are blocked when caller does not hold lease.
- [ ] Lease holder + health is visible in WarRoom UI/telemetry.
- [ ] Build passes: `cd apps/warroom && npm run build`.

## Delivery Artifact
Code changes in orchestrator/worker/runtime control paths, any required schema/migration updates, UI visibility updates for lease state, and updated handover docs describing lease behavior and failover.

## Handover Context (70% Guardrail Start)
Start by updating `docs/WARROOM_HANDOVER.md` with current actor roles and lifecycle assumptions. Keep the package concise and takeover-ready because context budget is treated as near 70%.

## Objective
Implement a permission matrix and lifecycle state machine so orchestration, admin override, and agent execution actions are explicit, validated, and auditable.

## Execution Prompt
1. Handover-first: document current role/action assumptions before edits.
2. Define explicit role-action matrix (orchestrator, admin override, worker, human operator).
3. Encode lifecycle transitions with allowed-from/allowed-to rules and rejection reasons.
4. Enforce checks in server actions and worker paths that mutate task/agent lifecycle.
5. Add audit metadata for denied transitions and manual override events.
6. Expose current lifecycle + permission state in WarRoom operator-facing UI.
7. Update handover docs with final matrix and transition table.

## Scope / Non-goals
Scope: role-action policy, lifecycle transition validation, denial/error semantics, and audit visibility.

- Stable, machine-neutral references in handoff and product docs.

## Context
WarRoom is intended to be portable and runnable in any developer/operator environment. ## Objective

## Scope / Non-goals
In scope:

- provide deterministic pass/fail output and remediation guidance.
- document how this preflight gates portability readiness.

Non-goals:
- full app containerization (handled in dependent issues).
- CI enforcement (handled in dependent issues).

- additive and reversible changes only.
- no breaking change to existing local non-Docker run path.
- align with board SSOT and existing readiness/runtime safety discipline.

## Acceptance Checks
- [ ] preflight command exists and runs from repository root.
- [ ] command validates Docker CLI + daemon + compose capability.
- [ ] output includes explicit remediation when failing.
- [ ] output contains no secret values.

- issue evidence showing pass/fail examples.

## Non-goals
- replace existing Homebrew/local Postgres path.

- false-positive health when daemon exists but compose plugin is missing.

Create a continuous tech-intelligence ingestion agent that gathers, normalizes, and deduplicates new agent/tool/model signals from prioritized public sources.

## Execution Prompt
Design an ingestion pipeline and agent workflow that monitors a configurable source catalog (for example Reddit communities, Hugging Face, OpenAI/Anthropic/Google/Meta blogs, and other official AI company update channels). ## Scope / Non-goals
In scope:
- source registry with per-source polling/crawl strategy.
- normalized discovery schema and deduplication logic.

Non-goals:

- closed/private source scraping without explicit authorization.
- replacing human approval boundaries.

- preserve auditability of source, timestamp, and normalization decisions.
- no secrets in logs/UI/docs.

## Acceptance Checks
- [ ] source registry supports expanding list of AI news/agent sources.
- [ ] ingestion produces normalized and deduplicated discovery items.
- [ ] each item stores source provenance and fetch timestamp.
- [ ] pipeline health and freshness are visible to operators.

## Delivery Artifact
- source-registry design and ingestion workflow spec.

Update the operational documentation set (`WARROOM_HANDOVER`, `WARROOM_STATUS_BRAINDUMP`, `NEXT_AGENT_PROMPT`, and `RULES`) with a mandatory incident-learning loop and a portability validation checklist that explicitly covers route checks and container log checks. Attach commit-level evidence and close only after board and docs are synchronized.

## Scope / Non-goals
In scope:
- incident record on the board with concrete evidence and remediation references.
- update handover/status/prompt/rules docs with incident-learning protocol.
- include deterministic verification steps for Docker runtime portability incidents.

Non-goals:
- new feature development outside incident prevention/documentation.
- changing lease/role/lifecycle/email-ingress invariants.

## Constraints
- board remains SSOT; status and evidence must be synchronized in the same work window.
- no secrets in issue comments, docs, logs, or output.
- additive/reversible changes only.
- preserve existing execution lane order for launch issues (#136 -> #133 -> #134+#137 -> #135 -> #138).

## Acceptance Checks
- [ ] A dedicated board issue exists documenting both regressions with RCA and fix evidence.
- [ ] `docs/WARROOM_HANDOVER.md` includes a current incident ledger entry and prevention checks.
- [ ] `docs/WARROOM_STATUS_BRAINDUMP.md` includes incident status and operational learning snapshot.
- [ ] `docs/NEXT_AGENT_PROMPT.md` includes mandatory incident-capture and verification steps.
- [ ] `docs/RULES.md` includes incident-learning governance requirements.
- [ ] Board status is moved through execution states and finalized with evidence comment.

## Delivery Artifact
- Closed bug issue with evidence links + updated operational docs codifying incident learning and prevention.

## Summary
Dockerized WarRoom regressions were fixed in code but not yet fully codified as board-level learning and mandatory operational standards.

## Steps to reproduce
1. Start WarRoom via Docker bootstrap in production mode.
2. Open `/signin` and `/agents`.
3. Observe server-component failures when runtime write path or `ps` portability assumptions break.

## Expected behaviour
Incidents are fully captured on board and in doctrine docs, with mandatory prevention checks for future runs.

## Actual behaviour
Fixes were applied and pushed, but formal board-linked incident learning and standards updates were incomplete.

**Product:** reply

## Objective
Deliver the first POC surface for Reply: a simple browser-based chat window on localhost where the user can type or paste a message and ask for suggested replies; suggestions are shown in the chat so the project can be tested before any messaging or cloud integration.

## Execution Prompt
1. Implement a small web app (or extend the existing reply repo) that serves a **chat UI** on **localhost** (e.g. Node/Express, Python Flask, or similar; or static + small API). No auth or public deployment required for POC.
2. The UI must include: a chat-style input (user can type or paste an incoming message), a way to request a reply suggestion (e.g. "Suggest reply" button or command), and a clear display of the suggested reply (or multiple options) in the chat.
3. Wire the UI to a reply-generation backend. For POC this can be: (a) the existing keyword-based ReplyEngine logic (e.g. via a thin API), or (b) a stub that returns mock suggestions until the knowledge pipeline is ready. Prefer (a) if the current ReplyEngine can be called from a local server.
4. Document in the reply repo how to start the server and open the chat in a browser (e.g. `http://localhost:3000` or similar). Ensure a single command or short steps to run the POC.
5. Update reply docs (e.g. README, POC_FIRST_FUNCTIONS.md or RELEASE_NOTES) to state that the localhost chat POC is implemented and how to run it.

## Scope / Non-goals
Scope: localhost-only chat UI, reply suggestion request/response in the browser, minimal backend to generate or stub suggestions, run instructions. Non-goals: Gmail/Drive/Notes integration in this issue, user accounts, production deployment, mobile app.

## Constraints
Run only on localhost. Do not expose secrets or credentials. Keep stack minimal (e.g. one language/runtime). If reusing the existing Swift ReplyEngine, expose it via a small local HTTP service or equivalent; do not require Xcode to run the POC chat.

## Acceptance Checks
- [ ] A chat-style UI is reachable in a browser at a localhost URL (e.g. http://localhost:3000).
- [ ] User can type or paste a message and trigger "suggest reply" (or equivalent).
- [ ] At least one suggested reply is shown in the chat (from real logic or stub).
- [ ] README or docs describe how to start the server and open the chat in one place.
- [ ] No TBD in the delivered code paths or docs for this POC.

## Delivery Artifact
- Working localhost chat app (code in reply repo or linked repo).
- Updated reply docs (README and/or POC/RELEASE_NOTES) with run instructions.
- Commit(s) and optional short evidence comment on this issue with the localhost URL and how to run.

**Product:** reply

## Objective
Enable Reply to use "how I act" by ingesting knowledge from the user's data. For the first POC: define a minimal knowledge model (what we store, how we index) and implement ingestion for at least one source (e.g. local text/markdown files or Gmail) so the system can annotate the reply engine with the user's style and context.

## Execution Prompt
1. **Design (docs):** In the reply repo, document a minimal "knowledge" model: what data we keep (e.g. snippets, labels, source), how we store it (e.g. in-memory, SQLite, or a small vector store), and how the reply engine can query it (e.g. by keyword or embedding). Target: Gmail, Google Drive, iCloud, Apple Notes, calendar events, and local files as future sources; POC implements one.
2. **Implement one ingestion pipeline:** Choose one source for POC: (a) **Local files** — scan a configurable folder (e.g. `./knowledge` or user-configurable path) for `.txt`/`.md` files, parse and index them into the knowledge store; or (b) **Gmail** — use Gmail API (with user OAuth) to read a bounded set of sent emails, extract text/snippets, and index. Implement only one for POC; document how to add more later.
3. **Wire to reply flow (optional for POC):** If the reply engine already has a "context" or "knowledge" hook, connect the ingested data so suggestions can use it. If not, at least expose a minimal API or function that returns "relevant snippets" for a given query so the chat UI or reply logic can consume it in a follow-up.
4. **Document:** In reply docs (e.g. POC_FIRST_FUNCTIONS.md, README, or a short INGESTION.md), describe the chosen source, how to run ingestion (e.g. script or CLI), and where data is stored. No secrets in repo; use env or config for API keys and paths.

## Scope / Non-goals
Scope: one ingestion source (local files or Gmail), minimal knowledge schema and storage, docs for design and run. Non-goals: full Gmail + Drive + iCloud + Notes + calendar in this issue, production-scale storage, UI for managing ingested data (CLI or script is enough for POC).

## Constraints
User data must stay local or in a user-controlled store. No hardcoded credentials. Prefer minimal dependencies. If using Gmail API, use official OAuth and document consent and scopes.

## Acceptance Checks
- [ ] A knowledge model (schema/storage) is documented in the reply repo.
- [ ] At least one ingestion path is implemented (local files or Gmail) and runnable via script/CLI.
- [ ] Ingested data is stored and queryable (e.g. "get snippets for query X") for use by the reply engine or chat.
- [ ] Docs describe how to run ingestion and where data lives; secrets and paths are configurable.
- [ ] No TBD in the delivered ingestion path or docs.

## Delivery Artifact
- Code and/or scripts in reply repo for knowledge model and one ingestion source.
- Docs: knowledge design, run instructions for ingestion, config/env for keys and paths.
- Commit(s) and evidence comment on this issue with how to run ingestion and one example query result.

**Product:** reply

## Objective
Reply can use Apple Notes as a knowledge source so chat suggestions are informed by the user's notes (how they write, what they care about). This is a Roadmap item: scope and dependencies first; breakdown into Backlog issues for implementation.

## Context
- Reply already has a knowledge model and local-file ingestion (#162). Chat returns snippets from ingested content.
- Apple Notes has no public API. Options for a local Node.js app: (1) AppleScript bridge via `osascript` — read and write; (2) SQLite read-only — `~/Library/Group Containers/group.com.apple.notes/NoteStore.sqlite`; (3) JXA. Writing to the DB directly is unsafe; AppleScript or JXA is recommended for writes. For "answer chat questions" we need **read** only.
- Dependencies: existing ingestion design and chat (#154, #162) are done. This adds a second source (source=apple-notes) into the same store or query path.

## Scope / Non-goals
- In scope: Read Apple Notes (iCloud/local account) from Node.js; produce snippets in the same schema as existing knowledge; wire into Reply chat so notes are used when suggesting replies.
- Non-goals: Writing to Apple Notes from Reply (optional later); iOS/ipadOS (macOS only for POC); MCP server or third-party apps (we do our own Node.js ingestion).

## Dependencies
- Depends on: #162 (knowledge model + local ingestion) — done.
- Blocks: Backlog issues that implement design and ingestion.

## Next steps
- Break down into Backlog: (1) Design Apple Notes in knowledge model + INGESTION.md; (2) Implement read ingestion (Node.js, AppleScript or SQLite); (3) Wire into ingest/chat and docs. Move to Ready when EPP is complete and delivery order set.

**Product:** reply

## Objective
Reply uses a **local agent** (Ollama) to **annotate** all ingested knowledge — tagging, summarizing, and structuring content so it can power a "digital me" for reply suggestions. Annotation runs on data we already ingest (local files) and will ingest (Apple Notes first; then other sources). No cloud LLM required; everything stays on the machine.

## Context
- **Ollama:** Installed (0.15.2). Available models include: `llama3.2:3b`, `deepseek-r1:7b`, `deepseek-r1:1.5b`, `qwen2.5:7b`, `llama3.1:8b`, `qwen3-coder:30b`, and others. Llama 3.2 3B is a good balance for document annotation; DeepSeek R1 for reasoning/recall.
- **Current ingestion:** Local files (#162) and (when done) Apple Notes (#163–#166). Snippets stored in `knowledge/store.json`; chat uses keyword match.
- **Goal:** After (or during) ingestion, a local Ollama model annotates each snippet (e.g. tags, short summary, key facts, tone). Annotated data is stored and used when generating reply suggestions so the system reflects "how I act" and what I care about. Design should consider RAG/vector DB later (e.g. ChromaDB) for semantic search; for POC, annotation can extend the existing snippet schema.
- **First source to consider for annotation:** Apple Notes (in addition to local files), so annotation design should suit note-like content (topics, summaries, structure).

## Scope / Non-goals
- In scope: Design and implement an annotation pipeline that calls Ollama on ingested snippets; store annotations; use them in reply suggestions. Apple Notes as first new source to support.
- Non-goals: Cloud APIs; full RAG/vector DB in the first slice (can be a later Backlog item); Dify/Mem0/Jan integration (we can use Ollama + Node.js directly for POC).

## Dependencies
- Depends on: existing knowledge model and ingestion (#162); Apple Notes design/ingestion (#164–#166) so Notes are in the store before or alongside annotation.
- Blocks: Backlog issues that implement design, Ollama integration, and wiring into chat.

## Next steps
- Break down into Backlog: (1) Design annotation (schema, when to run, which Ollama model(s), Apple Notes–aware); (2) Implement Ollama annotation (Node.js, call Ollama API or CLI, store annotations); (3) Wire annotation into ingest/chat and docs. Move to Ready when EPP is complete and delivery order set.

content.body:
**Product:** reply

## Objective
Implement a Node.js annotation step that calls **Ollama** on ingested snippets and stores annotations (e.g. tags, summary, key facts) so Reply can use them for "digital me"–style suggestions. Works on existing store (local files + Apple Notes once that ingestion exists).

## Scope
- **Ollama integration:** Use Ollama API (HTTP) or `ollama run` from Node.js. Model configurable via env (e.g. REPLY_ANNOTATION_MODEL, default e.g. llama3.2:3b). No new system deps beyond Ollama.
- **Annotation prompt:** For each snippet (or batch), send a short prompt asking the model to return structured annotation: tags, one-line summary, optional key facts. Output format: JSON or structured text we can parse.
- **Storage:** Extend existing store or add annotation store (e.g. per-snippet id: annotations). Document in design (#1). No cloud; all local.
- **Run:** Script or CLI (e.g. `node chat/annotate.js` or `npm run annotate`) after ingest; reads from knowledge store, calls Ollama, writes annotations back.
- **Apple Notes:** No special-case code required if snippets are already in the same store; annotation runs on all snippets. If Apple Notes have different structure, prompt can treat note title as path and body as text (already in schema).

## Non-goals
- Vector DB, embeddings, or RAG in this issue.
- Training or fine-tuning models.

## Dependencies
- Depends on: Design annotation (Backlog #1). Optional: Apple Notes ingestion (#166) so Notes are in the store; can run on local-file snippets first.

Context

Current behavior (2026-02-18)

- Counts: computed per contact via LanceDB `countRows(path LIKE <prefix>%)` across all handle aliases.
- Mode: `meta.mode = contactStore` (a previous attempt at a db-built index was too heavy / brittle).

- No broad refactor of unrelated runtime subsystems.

- Board is SSOT; update issue evidence and board status after meaningful steps.

- Recommendation rank includes trace fields for why items are boosted.

content.body:
## Objective
Enforce Reply manifesto: outbound messages are allowed only as replies on channel identities that have already contacted us.

## Manifesto Rule (strict)
- Reply is **reply-only**.
- We can send to a contact only on channels/addresses that have inbound proof from that same identity.
- Identity matching is channel-aware and address-level strict:
  - If inbound came from `email_a@example.com`, sending to `email_b@example.com` is blocked until `email_b@example.com` has inbound history.
  - Knowing an address from another channel profile (for example iMessage contact card) is not sufficient to unlock outbound on that address/channel.

## Scope
- Add outbound policy gate for channel + sender-identity allowlist based on observed inbound messages.
- Enforce in all send routes (email/iMessage/WhatsApp and future channels).
- Return explicit deny reason + remediation hint (for example "wait for inbound from this address/channel").
- Add audit log entry when policy blocks a send.

## Non-goals
- Auto-initiating first contact on new channels.
- Silent fallback to another channel when blocked.

## Acceptance criteria
- Outbound to unseen channel/address is denied by default.
- Outbound to previously inbound-verified channel/address is allowed (subject to existing approvals).
- Cross-address sends in same channel are blocked unless that exact address has inbound history.
- Tests cover multi-email contacts, alias addresses, and merged profiles.

## Dependencies
- Related: #200, #202, #199, #186, #185.
content.title: {reply}: Enforce reply-only outbound by inbound-verified channel identity

## Objective
Decompose the monolithic `chat/server.js` (~2,800 lines, 90+ functions) into focused route modules to improve maintainability, testability, and developer experience.

## Context
The global audit identified `server.js` as the single biggest code quality risk. All routing, business logic, database interactions, and external API integrations are in one file. This makes it impossible to unit test individual routes and creates high risk of merge conflicts.

## Scope
**IN:** Extract route handlers into separate modules (e.g., `routes/suggest.js`, `routes/kyc.js`, `routes/sync.js`, `routes/settings.js`, `routes/gmail.js`, `routes/channel-bridge.js`). Keep `server.js` as the thin entry point (imports, middleware, routing table).

**OUT:** No new features. No API changes. No database changes.

## Dependencies
- #199 (Done) — security baseline in place
- ESLint + test framework already set up

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `server.js` < 300 lines (routing table + middleware only)
- [ ] Each route module is independently `require()`-able
- [ ] `npm test` still passes (26+ tests)
- [ ] `node chat/security-audit.js` → critical=0 warn=0
- [ ] All API endpoints functional (manual smoke test)
